kpi_cards.label: LME CASH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: Fri 17-Jul OFFICIAL (Westmetall): cash $3,154.00, −$16.00 (−0.50%) vs Thu — a week that peaked at $3,171 (Tue) and eased back to consolidate near $3,150. This is a clean, settled close: today is Saturday, LME closed, next ring Mon 20-Jul. ~18.2% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855).

kpi_cards.label: LME 3-MONTH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: 17-Jul official $3,146.50 (−$20.00, −0.63%); on the week barely changed (vs $3,153 on 10-Jul, −0.2%). But the cash–3M spread WIDENED to +$7.50 backwardation from +$3.50 — prompt tightness intensified even as the flat price softened. Resistance $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382); $3,061.5 the structural pivot.

kpi_cards.label: LME STOCK (t)
kpi_cards.delta: 17-Jul official 280,100 t (−1,500 on the day) — the unbroken drawdown since early April (509,250 t on 02-Jan → 280,100, −45.0% YTD) leaves the thinnest visible cover of the cycle. Falling stocks + widening backwardation are the physical tightening signal running against a softer flat price.

kpi_cards.label: BRENT ($/bbl)
kpi_cards.delta: Brent rose to ~$85.95 Fri (+2.04%), up more than 10% on the week as US–Iran strikes peaked mid-week before easing Thursday evening. Energy is the largest smelter cost line; the war premium in oil has not unwound even as diplomacy (Qatar / Vance / Kushner / Witkoff) gains traction.

benchmark.name: LME Cash settlement
benchmark.note: Fri 17-Jul OFFICIAL (Westmetall): $3,154.00 vs 16-Jul $3,170.00 = −$16.00 (−0.50%). The week swung from $3,171 (Tue) to $3,154 (Fri) — net flat vs $3,156 a week earlier; the market is consolidating around $3,150. avg = June-2026 monthly mean ($3,458.6).

benchmark.name: LME 3-Month
benchmark.note: 17-Jul official $3,146.50 vs 16-Jul $3,166.50 (−$20.00, −0.63%); vs 10-Jul $3,153.00 −$6.50 on the week. Resistance stack: $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382 of the June–July decline); a close above $3,260 upgrades the technical toward $3,330–3,400. avg = June-2026 3M mean ($3,438.9).

benchmark.name: Cash–3M spread
benchmark.note: BACKWARDATION WIDENED to +$7.50 (cash $3,154.00 over 3M $3,146.50) from +$3.50 on 16-Jul and +$3.00 on 15-Jul — three straight sessions of building prompt tightness on a 280,100 t stock base (−45.0% YTD). The front end is tightening while the flat price consolidates: the classic tight-prompt / soft-outright configuration.

premiums.name: MJP Q3 (Main Japanese Port)
premiums.src: Q3-2026 SETTLED at $395/t (S&P Global Platts, assessed 06-Jul) — a decade high (+12% q/q vs Q2's $350–353). SMM flags spot tightness and squeeze risk under the LME's deep backwardation. Q4 talks (~Sep) open against the decade-high anchor; the swing variable is whether the Hormuz disruption persists or the US–Iran diplomacy (MoU to ~18-Aug) restores flow. Source: Platts via IndexBox. avg = Q1-26 settlement ($195).

premiums.name: CIF Japan spot (MJP spot)
premiums.src: Spot firming toward the Q3 settlement: Platts CIF Japan spot last publicly at ~$370/t (06-Jul); SMM's 'squeeze risk climbs under LME deep backwardation' analysis flags tight spot supply pushing the QMJP premium up. Point ~$365/t. Verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0343 / SMM weekly.

premiums.name: European Duty Unpaid (EDU)
premiums.src: Platts P1020A duty-UNPAID in-whs Rotterdam last publicly assessed ~$525–560/t (mid $542.50, 03-Jul) — no fresher public print. Structural floor: 6% EU duty + CBAM + Mozal (~580 ktpa) on care & maintenance. Gulf war-risk freight is the live swing leg; the toll threat died 14-Jul and diplomacy is now easing the tail-risk (S&P Global / SMM).

premiums.name: Billets Rotterdam Duty-Paid
premiums.src: Value-added 6063 billet conversion premium, in-whs duty-paid Rotterdam — last public reference (~late-Jun; no fresh public print). Conversion premia are stickier than ingot premia; Mozal remains down. Verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0002.

premiums.name: MW US Transaction (Platts)
premiums.src: Platts US MW Transaction premium last publicly assessed 108.7¢/lb ≈ $2,396/t (03-Jul). The all-in US delivered price embeds the 50% Section-232 tariff (≈$1,577/t at the 17-Jul cash of $3,154), so the policy leg dominates. Alcoa's Q2 flagged Section-232 on Canadian metal rising ~$35m q/q. Verify S&P Global.

premiums.name: US Midwest Duty-Paid (P1020A)
premiums.src: SMM weekly DDP print 109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t (10-Jul). Duty-paid = LME + Section-232 tariff (50%) + market premium; the policy leg is ~two-thirds of the all-in. The tariff-$ component eased ~$8/t with Friday's softer LME. Verify Platts/Fastmarkets.

macro.name: Brent crude ($/bbl)
macro.note: Rose to ~$85.95 Fri (+2.04%), up more than 10% on the week. US–Iran strikes peaked mid-week (the most intense since the mid-June ceasefire) before easing Thursday evening; the strait remains physically disrupted (no southern-route transits with transponders on since 07-Jul, Lloyd's List). Energy is the largest smelter cost line.

macro.name: US Dollar Index (DXY)
macro.note: DXY ~100.7, roughly flat Friday after a soft-inflation week (CPI −0.4% 14-Jul, PPI −0.3% 15-Jul). July Fed-hike odds ~20%, September ~60% (swaps). A softer dollar removes the headwind that capped metals — supportive at the margin even as fundamentals soften.

macro.name: EUR/USD
macro.note: Euro ~1.147, steady on a flat dollar. The ECB still carries a war-energy inflation problem with Brent near $86. A firmer euro lifts EU-denominated smelter power and conversion costs.

macro.name: USD/CNY
macro.note: Yuan steady ~6.78. June data stayed aluminium-bearish: RECORD unwrought-aluminium exports of 711 kt (+12.5% m/m; H1 3.4 Mt, +16.3% y/y — GACC) against Q2 GDP of 4.3% y/y (slowest since 2022) and retail sales +1.0% — production strong, domestic demand weak, exports the release valve.

macro.name: USD/JPY
macro.note: Yen ~162, little changed. A still-weak yen cushions Japan-bound cargo premia in local terms; the binding Q4-talks variable remains Gulf transit risk and the durability of the US–Iran de-escalation.

macro.name: US 10Y yield (%)
macro.note: 10Y ~4.55% after two cool inflation prints (CPI −0.4%, PPI −0.3%) pulled July hike odds to ~20%. BoC held at 2.25% Wed. Real rates remain the macro cap on metals; a hot data surprise re-arms the dollar.

macro_events: WEEK IN REVIEW: aluminium consolidated near $3,150 (17-Jul official cash $3,154 / 3M $3,146.5) as two cool US inflation prints (CPI −0.4% 14-Jul, PPI −0.3% 15-Jul) and record Chinese exports offset a peaking-then-easing Hormuz conflict. The cash–3M backwardation WIDENED to +$7.50 across the week even as the flat price softened — prompt tightness intensifying, LME stock −45.0% YTD.

GEOPOLITICS PIVOTED TOWARD DIPLOMACY: after the most intense US strikes since the mid-June ceasefire, the flare-up eased Thursday evening. Qatar is mediating and VP Vance, Jared Kushner and envoy Witkoff are working the channels; the 60-day US–Iran MoU (Article 5 meant to reopen the strait) runs to ~18-Aug. Fragile, but the war-premium's staying power is now in question.

STRAIT STILL DISRUPTED PHYSICALLY: Lloyd's List reports no large vessels transited the southern (Omani) route with transponders on since 07-Jul; Kpler shows crossings down sharply. Brent still rose to ~$85.95 (+2.04% Fri, >10% on the week). Diplomacy is advancing while the waterway remains effectively closed to visible traffic.

ALCOA Q2 (reported 16-Jul after close): revenue $3.97bn (+24% q/q, highest in nearly a decade); adj. EPS $2.12 MISSED the ~$2.31–2.32 consensus; adj. EBITDA $901m; the Aluminum segment posted a RECORD $1.073bn EBITDA at a 32.3% margin — but higher costs (incl. ~$35m more Section-232 on Canadian metal) drove the miss and the shares fell.
CHINA: Q2 GDP +4.3% y/y (slowest since 2022) with June IP +5.3% but retail sales +1.0% — production-heavy, demand-light, the mix that keeps the record 711 kt monthly export valve open (NBS/GACC).
lme_commentary: The week resolved into consolidation with a tightening front end. The 17-Jul official printed cash $3,154.00 (−0.50%) / 3M $3,146.50 (−0.63%), leaving the flat price essentially unchanged on the week (vs $3,156 cash a week earlier) after a mid-week peak at $3,171. The signal to watch is the curve: the cash–3M spread widened to +$7.50 BACKWARDATION — three consecutive sessions of building prompt tightness (+$3.00 → +$3.50 → +$7.50) on a 280,100 t stock base (−45.0% YTD, the thinnest cover of the cycle). Against that physical tightening sit two offsets: a disinflationary US macro (CPI −0.4%, PPI −0.3%) and a wide-open Chinese export valve (record 711 kt in June). The new variable is diplomacy — the US–Iran flare-up eased Thursday evening and Qatar-brokered talks are live against a ~18-Aug MoU deadline, even as the strait stays physically shut to visible traffic. Technically, the market must hold the $3,138 shelf and reclaim $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382) to validate a catch-up to the backwardation; a break of $3,061.5 negates the recovery. Next LME ring: Monday 20-Jul.

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (SMM/Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: ~$338/t FOB East Australia — latest public reference; the surplus story is intact (EGA's 2.4 Mtpa Al Taweelah refinery restarted 10-Jul; oversupply persists). Delivered cargoes into the Gulf still carry war-risk cover, though the easing US–Iran conflict trims the tail. Verify SMM/Fastmarkets.

alumina.name: Alumina API / Platts region
alumina.src: Ex-China benchmark region ~$303/t — easing on the building surplus (Q1 avg $306.9; latest public reference). SMM flags ex-China metallurgical-grade output down ~6% y/y in June — a partial offset to the EGA restart. Verify Platts.

alumina.name: SHFE alumina futures (AO2609)
alumina.src: Last public futures print in the ¥2,700s (SMM); price below moving averages, port stocks building. SMM domestic spot weighted average ~¥2,550/t. The strait squeeze still weighs on Gulf refinery feed (~60% of regional alumina imports transit Hormuz) even as the global balance stays long (SMM).

alumina.name: Platts FOB Australia (futures)
alumina.src: Alumina FOB Australia (Platts) futures ~$308/t — CME/TradingView; no material move in the latest public print.

alumina_notes: The alumina market remains the mirror image of metal: globally LOOSENING (EGA's 2.4 Mtpa restart, Guinea bauxite oversupply ~$68/dmt CIF China, Chinese port stocks building; domestic China spot ~¥2,550 and drifting) — ex-China consolidation in the $300–340 band holds.

The regional wrinkle is easing at the margin: the Hormuz disruption still prices war-risk cover into the ~60% of Gulf alumina imports that transit the strait, but the US–Iran de-escalation (diplomacy live, MoU to ~18-Aug) has taken the acute-escalation premium off the top. SMM separately flags ex-China metallurgical-grade output down ~6% y/y in June.

Guinea's pending export-quota policy (targeting >$100/dmt bauxite) remains the one clear bullish catalyst on the commodity leg; record Australian and Metro Mining shipments underline how long the seaborne market currently is.
net_read: TIGHTENING FRONT END, CONSOLIDATING OUTRIGHT. The 17-Jul official left aluminium near $3,150 (cash $3,154, −0.50%) but the cash–3M backwardation WIDENED to +$7.50 — three straight sessions of building prompt tightness on the thinnest stocks of the cycle (−45.0% YTD). That physical endorsement of the supply squeeze runs against a friendlier-for-demand-but-heavier-for-supply backdrop: two soft US inflation prints and a record 711 kt of Chinese June exports. The wildcard shifted this week from escalation to diplomacy — the US–Iran flare-up eased Thursday evening with Qatar mediating against a ~18-Aug MoU, though the strait stays shut to visible traffic. Net: the burden of proof is balanced. A backwardation that keeps building with premiums firming validates the path to $3,230/$3,260; a diplomatic breakthrough that reopens the strait, landing on top of record Chinese metal, tilts it toward a fade to $3,085/$3,061.5. Alcoa's Q2 (record aluminium margins, EPS miss on tariff/cost drag) frames the producer reality. Situational awareness, not advice.

feed.text: WEEKEND WRAP: markets closed. Latest LME official is Fri 17-Jul — Al cash $3,154.00 / 3M $3,146.50 / stock 280,100 t; cash–3M +$7.50 backwardation (widened from +$3.50). Aluminium net flat on the week but the front end tightened three sessions running. Next ring Mon 20-Jul.

BACKWARDATION BUILDS: the 17-Jul official widened the cash–3M spread to +$7.50 while stock fell to 280,100 t (−45.0% YTD). Prompt tightness intensified even as the flat price eased −$16 — the physical squeeze signal strengthening under the Hormuz disruption (Westmetall).

OIL FIRM: Brent rose ~$85.95 (+2.04%), up more than 10% on the week. Lloyd's List reports no large vessels on the southern (Omani) route with transponders on since 07-Jul — the strait stays effectively shut to visible traffic even as strikes eased (CNBC / Lloyd's List).

DE-ESCALATION SIGNAL: the week's US strikes — the most intense since the mid-June ceasefire — eased Thursday evening. Qatar is mediating; VP Vance, Jared Kushner and envoy Witkoff are working the channels toward reviving the MoU (Article 5 = reopen the strait), which runs to ~18-Aug (CBS / CSMonitor).

ALCOA Q2 MISS DESPITE RECORD MARGINS: revenue $3.97bn (+24% q/q, best in nearly a decade) and a RECORD Aluminum-segment EBITDA of $1.073bn (32.3% margin), but adj. EPS $2.12 missed the ~$2.31 consensus on higher costs (incl. ~$35m more Section-232 on Canadian metal); shares fell (BusinessWire / Investing.com).

SECOND SOFT US PRINT: June PPI −0.3% m/m (final-demand goods −1.4%, gasoline ~−12%) after Tuesday's CPI −0.4% — disinflation confirmed, July hike odds ~20%, dollar softer. Friendlier for risk, but for aluminium the offset is a wide-open Chinese export valve (BLS / CNBC).

CHINA Q2 GDP +4.3% y/y — slowest since 2022; June IP +5.3% beat, retail +1.0% soft. Production-heavy, demand-light: the mix behind the record 711 kt monthly export flow into the ex-China market (NBS via CNBC/FXStreet).

news.headline: CONSOLIDATION WITH A TIGHTENING CURVE: the 17-Jul official left aluminium near $3,150 (cash $3,154, −0.50%) but the cash–3M backwardation widened to +$7.50 (from +$3.50) on a 280,100 t stock base (−45.0% YTD). Flat price soft, front end tightening.

STRIKES EASE, DIPLOMACY ADVANCES: after the most intense US strikes since the June ceasefire, the flare-up eased Thursday evening. Qatar is mediating; Vance/Kushner/Witkoff work the channels; the MoU (Article 5 = reopen strait) runs to ~18-Aug. Strait still shut to visible traffic; Brent ~$86.

ALCOA Q2 MISS ON COSTS: revenue $3.97bn (best in ~a decade) and record Aluminum-segment EBITDA ($1.073bn, 32.3% margin), but adj. EPS $2.12 missed ~$2.31 on higher costs incl. ~$35m more Section-232 on Canadian metal; shares fell. The tariff cuts both ways — lifts US premia, taxes cross-border metal.

TWO SOFT US PRINTS: June PPI −0.3% after CPI −0.4% — disinflation confirmed, July hike odds ~20%, dollar softer. Friendlier for risk, but aluminium's offset is the record Chinese export valve, so the net flat-price impulse is muted.

RECORD EXPORTS, WEAK GROWTH: China's June aluminium exports hit a record 711 kt (first >700 kt; H1 3.4 Mt, +16.3% y/y) while Q2 GDP slowed to 4.3%. More surplus metal into the ex-China market as it tightens on Gulf disruption.

earnings.headline: Revenue $3.97bn (+24% q/q, highest in nearly a decade); adj. EPS $2.12 (MISS vs ~$2.31 consensus); adj. net income $562m; adj. EBITDA $901m (from $595m in Q1); Aluminum segment RECORD EBITDA $1.073bn at 32.3% margin.
earnings.readthrough: First producer P&L under the Hormuz-disruption regime: record aluminium margins and near-decade-high revenue on higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums, but adj. EPS missed on higher costs — notably ~$35m more Section-232 tariff on Canadian metal into the US — and the shares fell. South32 upstream acquisition ($4.1bn upfront, up to $5.6bn contingent to 2030) progressing, close expected H1-2027. The tariff is a double edge: it inflates US premia but taxes Alcoa's cross-border Canadian metal.

earnings.headline: Adj. EBITDA NOK 8,668m (vs 9,516m yr-ago); adj. EPS NOK 2.07 (beat, up from 1.63).
earnings.readthrough: Primary Al EBITDA +2.7% YoY as Norwegian smelters reopened; soft alumina prices & stronger NOK; Extrusions outlook cut. Q2 due Wed 22-Jul (07:00 CEST) — the next European producer read on power costs and premium realisation.

earnings.headline: Adj. EPS $1.63 (beat $1.56); revenue $649m; net income $327m (incl. $288m Hawesville sale gain).
earnings.readthrough: Higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums; Q2 adj. EBITDA guided $315–335m.

earnings.headline: Net profit $200.3m (+316% YoY); EPS 53 fils; sales −17% on Gulf supply-chain disruption.
earnings.readthrough: LME averaged $3,195/t (+22% YoY) drove profit surge; volumes hit by regional conflict; ~19% capacity in controlled shutdown — restart path improves post-truce.

earnings.headline: Net profit SAR 1.63bn (~$436m, +5.5% YoY); revenue SAR 8.79bn (+3.2%); EBITDA $964m.
earnings.readthrough: Strong pricing across commodities; partly offset by lower phosphate volumes; 2026 capex set >$4bn.

earnings.headline: H1 copper-equivalent output +3% y/y; aluminium division improved; strong bauxite recovery; FY aluminium guidance UNCHANGED at 3.25–3.45 Mt; Pilbara Q2 shipments 89 Mt (+5%).
earnings.readthrough: First upstream print of the Q2 season (this morning): Western volumes are intact — the Gulf disruption is expressing itself in prices, freight and premia rather than output. Kitimat/AP60 ramp continues; H1 financial results due 13-Aug.

earnings.headline: Hillside Al ~538kt (9M, FY26 guide 720kt); Brazil Alumina record 1,060kt (9M); Worsley steady.
earnings.readthrough: Alumina sales −15% q/q on availability/timing; annual guidance intact despite cyclone gas disruption.

earnings.headline: Hindalco PAT ₹5,283cr (+66% YoY); India Al upstream EBITDA margin ~46%; Novelis FY26 adj. EBITDA $1.6bn (−9%).
earnings.readthrough: Record India operations; Novelis pressured by Oswego outage (~$104m) and tariffs (~$143m).

earnings.headline: Net profit guided RMB 5.30–5.59bn (+50–58% YoY).
earnings.readthrough: Strong on higher Al prices; China output near self-imposed 45 Mt capacity cap.

earnings.headline: Underlying profit $6.2bn (+>20%); underlying EBITDA $15.5bn (+25%, 58% margin); interim div 73c.
earnings.readthrough: Copper-led strength; broad base-metals read-across positive for the complex.

earnings.headline: FY25 results; copper & aluminium average prices +9% YoY; zinc metal +4%.
earnings.readthrough: Resilient structural demand for Cu/Al cited; broadly constructive sector tone.

earnings.headline: FY25 net loss $455m (vs $803m profit in 2024) on higher costs & lower output.
earnings.readthrough: Analysts expect stronger H1 2026 on higher prices; Asia pivot amid fractured trade flows.

earnings.headline: Guides H1-26 net profit ¥1.8–1.95bn, +154.4–175.6% YoY.
earnings.readthrough: Credits industry-chain cost reduction, aluminium prices fluctuating at highs, high-value-added deep-processing mix and energy-saving retrofits — the first sizeable China H1 pre-announcement of the season and a positive read-across for Chinese producer margins.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each listed peer's most recent reported results as of 18-Jul (public, listed-company information). Q2 season is OPEN: Alcoa reported 16-Jul — record Aluminum-segment margins (EBITDA $1.073bn, 32.3%) and near-decade-high revenue ($3.97bn), but adj. EPS $2.12 MISSED consensus (~$2.31) on higher costs including ~$35m more Section-232 tariff on Canadian metal; shares fell. Rio Tinto's production report (15-Jul) showed volumes intact and aluminium guidance unchanged (3.25–3.45 Mt). Next: Norsk Hydro Q2 Wed 22-Jul (07:00 CEST); Maaden (1211) H1 late-Jul/early-Aug (Tadawul). Context: Q2 average LME cash ≈ $3,459 (June mean) vs $3,154 at the latest official — sequentially strong realised prices with a softening tail. The disclosures that matter most: Gulf insurance/freight and US Midwest premium realisation under the 50% tariff.

commercial.text: 17-Jul official cash $3,154 (−0.50%), net flat on the week, but the cash–3M backwardation built to +$7.50 across three sessions on −45.0% YTD stocks. The discipline: watch whether the front-end tightening pulls the outright up (reclaim $3,230 MA20 → $3,260 .382) or whether a diplomatic strait-reopening plus record Chinese metal fades it to $3,085/$3,061.5. $3,061.5 the structural invalidation. Technical context, not advice.

MJP Q3 settled $395 (decade high); CIF Japan spot firming ~$365 on squeeze risk under the deep backwardation; US MW DDP ~109.75¢ ($2,420) with the tariff leg (~$1,577/t at 50% × $3,154) dominating the all-in. Alcoa's Q2 confirmed the tariff is a ~$35m q/q cost on Canadian metal even as it inflates US premia. Watch Q4 MJP talks (~Sep) against the $395 anchor and the durability of the Hormuz disruption.

Alumina stays globally long (EGA ramping, FOB Australia ~$338, API ~$303, China spot ~¥2,550) and Rio's Q2 confirmed upstream volumes intact (aluminium guidance 3.25–3.45 Mt). Gulf-delivered costs are easing at the margin as the US–Iran conflict de-escalates, though war-risk cover still prices into ~60% of regional alumina import routings. Secondary feed stays the quiet tightener (UAE scrap export ban, EU tariff changes). Watch index-vs-delivered divergence.

Re-set the cost stack to the de-escalation case: (1) war-risk quotes stay wide but the acute-escalation spike is fading as strikes ease and Qatar-brokered diplomacy advances toward the ~18-Aug MoU; (2) the 20% strait toll is DEAD — keep it out of delivered-cost scenarios; (3) the strait is still physically shut to visible traffic (no southern-route transits since 07-Jul), so interdiction/deviation risk persists cargo-by-cargo. The trend is toward normalisation but the waterway is not yet open — keep force-majeure and war-risk clauses live.

bottom_line: On 18-Jul the market closes the week consolidating near $3,150 with a tightening front end. The 17-Jul official (cash $3,154, −0.50%) left the flat price net flat on the week, but the cash–3M backwardation widened to +$7.50 across three straight sessions on the thinnest stocks of the cycle (−45.0% YTD) — prompt tightness is intensifying even as the outright softens. Nothing has converged on the fundamentals: physical squeeze (backwardation, drawn stocks, Gulf disruption) pulls one way; two soft US inflation prints and a record 711 kt of Chinese June exports pull the other. The week's decisive shift was geopolitical — the US–Iran flare-up eased Thursday evening and Qatar-brokered diplomacy is now live against a ~18-Aug MoU deadline, even though the strait remains shut to visible traffic. Alcoa's Q2 (record aluminium margins, EPS miss on tariff/cost drag) frames the producer reality. The tell for next week: does the backwardation keep building through Monday's ring, or does a diplomatic reopening begin to unwind the war premium?
sources: London Metal Exchange via Westmetall — westmetall.com (Al cash $3,154.00 / 3M $3,146.50 / stock 280,100 t; Cu/Ni/Zn/Pb tables same source, latest published 16-Jul). June monthly means from the same table.

CBS News — US-Iran ceasefire talks / new strikes; CSMonitor — 'Hormuz flare-up signals lasting fragility of US-Iran ceasefire'; CNN live updates; Wikipedia '2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis' / '2026 Iran war ceasefire'. MoU (Article 5 = reopen strait) runs to ~18-Aug.
Lloyd's List via CBS/NPR: no large vessels transited the southern (Omani) route with transponders on since 07-Jul; Kpler crossings down sharply.

Alcoa Corporation Q2 2026 release (news.alcoa.com; BusinessWire) — revenue $3.97bn, adj. EPS $2.12 (miss vs ~$2.31), adj. EBITDA $901m, Aluminum segment record EBITDA $1.073bn / 32.3% margin; Section-232 on Canadian metal +~$35m q/q. Investing.com / TradingView coverage.
BLS — CPI −0.4% m/m (14-Jul) and PPI −0.3% m/m (15-Jul); CNBC. Swap-implied July hike odds ~20%.
GACC via Reuters — June aluminium exports record 711 kt (H1 3.4 Mt, +16.3%); NBS via CNBC — Q2 GDP +4.3%, June IP +5.3%, retail +1.0%.
Trading Economics / CNBC — Brent ~$85.95 (+2.04% Fri), up >10% on the week; DXY ~100.7 flat.
S&P Global Platts Q3-2026 MJP settled $395/t (assessed 06-Jul), via IndexBox / Hellenic Shipping News.
SMM — 'Tight Spot Supply Pushes Up QMJP Premium… Squeeze Risk Climbs Under LME Deep Backwardation'; Platts CIF Japan spot ~$370/t (06-Jul).
Federal Register — 'Adjusting Imports of Aluminum and Steel into the United States' (50% rate).
European Commission — Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (6% MFN duty + CBAM).
Shanghai Metals Market (SMM) — news.metal.com; Fastmarkets. Premium/alumina prints as marked; subscription indices should be verified for any transaction.
caveats: Report refreshed Sat 18-Jul-2026 — LME closed for the weekend. The latest PUBLISHED official is Fri 17-Jul (cash $3,154.00 / 3M $3,146.50 / stock 280,100 t — Westmetall); this is a clean, settled close, not an intraday estimate. Next LME ring: Monday 20-Jul.

The base-metals board carries the latest published LME officials: aluminium on 17-Jul; copper, nickel, zinc and lead on 16-Jul (their 17-Jul officials were not yet posted on Westmetall at run time). Day % is each metal's latest-session move; YTD is vs the 02-Jan-2026 3M. Note nickel jumped +3.8% on 16-Jul — the standout cross-metal move of the week.

Premiums, alumina and the overseas physical complex show the latest PUBLIC prints (MJP Q3 settlement 06-Jul; SMM/Platts marks early-to-mid Jul). They lag the fast-moving Gulf logistics picture; subscription indices (Platts/Fastmarkets/SMM) should be verified for any transaction.

Everything published here is PUBLIC market information only — no confidential, internal or company-specific data. The only company-specific mentions are public listed-company facts in the peer-earnings table (Alcoa, Norsk Hydro, Century, Rio Tinto, Maaden (1211), etc.). Nothing here is trading, hedging or investment advice; Elliott Wave and technical notes are analytical context, not recommendations.

Geopolitical facts (US–Iran strikes and de-escalation, Qatar mediation, the ~18-Aug MoU, Hormuz traffic) are compiled from public reporting (CBS/CNN/CSMonitor/NPR/Lloyd's List) and evolve over the weekend; figures were current at ~08:00 London on 18-Jul and may have moved by the time of reading.

forward_path.basis: Latest PUBLISHED official (Westmetall); −0.50% vs 16-Jul — net flat on the week after a $3,171 mid-week peak. The cash–3M spread widened to +$7.50 backwardation. Next official Mon 20-Jul.
Latest published official; −0.63% vs 16-Jul; front spread +$7.50 BACKWARDATION (from +$3.50). Resistance $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382).

outlook.curve_note: The diagnostic strengthened. The cash–3M spread widened to +$7.50 BACKWARDATION on the 17-Jul official — three straight sessions of building prompt tightness (+$3.00 → +$3.50 → +$7.50) on a 280,100 t stock base (−45.0% YTD). The physical front end is tightening even as the flat price consolidates near $3,150 — a tight-prompt / soft-outright configuration that historically precedes either a flat-price catch-up (if tightness persists) or a spread collapse (if the driver — here, the Hormuz disruption — resolves). The counterweight is quantified at record scale: China exported 711 kt in June (first month ever >700 kt) into 4.3% GDP growth — the export valve is wide open. The new swing factor is diplomacy: the US–Iran flare-up eased Thursday evening and Qatar-brokered talks are live against a ~18-Aug MoU, though the strait stays shut to visible traffic. The 2026 consensus band ($3,200–3,500 averages) and the ~−365 kt structural deficit remain the floor beneath the argument.

consensus.note: Running above the World Bank base
First structural deficit in ~20 years; BofA flags near-term downside

balance.label: BofA/Morgan/BNP structural deficit; Goldman flags later surplus risk

capacity_pipeline.change: Alumina refinery restarted 10-Jul (ramping through H2, full capability targeted end-2026); smelter rebuild ahead of schedule
~19% capacity in controlled shutdown through the Gulf disruption; restart path improves as US–Iran diplomacy advances
Output near the 45 Mt self-imposed cap; June unwrought-aluminium exports a RECORD 711 kt (+12.5% m/m; H1 3.4 Mt, +16.3% y/y); Q2 GDP 4.3% — production strong, domestic demand weak
capacity_pipeline.timing: Structural ceiling on output; record export flow

capacity_pipeline.change: New smelter capacity ramping (BofA flags as added supply)
Q2 production report (15-Jul): H1 CuEq +3%, aluminium guidance intact 3.25–3.45 Mt, bauxite recovering; AP60/Kitimat incremental low-carbon capacity

scenarios.drivers: The Hormuz disruption persists — diplomacy stalls, the strait stays shut, and the widening backwardation drags the outright up on a sub-280kt stock base while a soft dollar helps
drivers_list: Geopolitics: the ~18-Aug MoU is not implemented, the southern corridor stays unusable, and interdiction/deviation risk keeps ~25% of ex-China supply hostage
Curve confirmation: the +$7.50 backwardation keeps BUILDING on subsequent officials — the tight-prompt configuration (backwardation + stocks −45.0% YTD) precedes flat-price catch-up
Macro tailwind: two soft US prints (CPI −0.4%, PPI −0.3%) cut July hike odds to ~20% and softened the dollar — the rates ceiling that capped rallies has lifted for now
Premiums: MJP spot firms toward/above the $395 Q3 settlement on squeeze risk; Q4 talks (~Sep) anchor high
Targets: $3,230/$3,260 on confirmation; a close above $3,260 opens $3,330–3,400; $3,600+ only on a sustained (30-day+) strait closure

scenarios.drivers: Consolidation resolves gradually — diplomacy delivers a managed-transit equilibrium, cargo moves at elevated but insurable cost, and record Chinese exports cap the war premium
drivers_list: Geopolitics: the Qatar-brokered track matures into a corridor arrangement around the MoU; sporadic risk keeps a front-of-curve premium without stopping flow
Supply: the ~−365 kt 2026 deficit persists but repairs visibly — EGA alumina back, record 711 kt June Chinese exports, Indonesia ramping, Rio volumes intact
China: Q2 GDP 4.3% with retail +1.0% — domestic softness keeps the export valve open; destocking supports the SHFE bottom but not a breakout
Macro: CPI/PPI relief holds but September stays ~60% priced — DXY ~100–101, neither capping nor fuelling the metal
Premiums: firm modestly on residual war-risk; Q4 MJP negotiates in the $340–410 band around the $395 anchor

scenarios.drivers: De-escalation succeeds or the macro re-shocks — a strait reopening unwinds the war premium into record supply, or hot data re-arms the hike trade and the dollar
drivers_list: Geopolitics: the MoU is implemented and the strait reopens (Article 5) — an Iranian 'strait open' statement or agreed Oman corridor restores flow; the June pattern (war premium unwinding in days) repeats
Macro: a hot data surprise re-prices September toward a done deal — DXY back through 101.5 and metals de-rate as a complex
Supply: the record export machine (711 kt June, H1 +16.3%) keeps metal flowing into the off-season; EGA beats its ramp; premium weeklies print lower
Curve: the +$7.50 backwardation collapses back toward flat/contango — the signal that the front end was pricing a disruption that has resolved
Technical: a daily close below $3,061.5 re-opens $2,990–3,020, then the .618 at $2,894

risks.risk: Hormuz disruption persists: strait shut to visible traffic (no southern-route transits since 07-Jul), diplomacy fragile (~25% of ex-China supply routes exposed)
Rapid de-escalation succeeds: the ~18-Aug MoU is implemented / strait reopens — war premium unwinds in days
Physical looseness at record scale: China June exports 711 kt (first >700 kt), Q2 GDP miss, EGA restart, off-season demand
Front-end squeeze: cash–3M backwardation building (+$7.50) on −45.0% YTD stocks — delivery/financing stress risk
Disinflation entrenches: two soft US prints (CPI −0.4%, PPI −0.3%) cut July hike odds to ~20%; softer dollar supports metals but signals demand weakness
US Section-232 at 50% distorts trade flows and premium realisation (tariff leg ≈$1,577/t; a ~$35m q/q cost drag on Alcoa's Canadian metal)

catalysts.event: Next LME ring — does the +$7.50 backwardation keep BUILDING, and does the flat price hold the $3,138 shelf / attack $3,230 (MA20)?
Norsk Hydro Q2 (07:00 CEST) — European producer read: power costs, Extrusions demand, premium realisation
US–Iran MoU deadline (Article 5 = reopen the Strait of Hormuz) — the pivotal de-escalation / re-escalation trigger for the war premium
Maaden (1211) H1 results (Tadawul) — Gulf producer read on realised prices and volumes through the disruption
MJP Q4 settlement talks open against the $395 decade-high anchor — the pending-quarter range ($345–425) resolves on strait duration and the Q3 spot-vs-settlement gap
A Qatar-brokered corridor mechanism or Iranian 'strait open' statement is the fastest unwind trigger; a breakdown of talks with fresh strikes is the fastest escalation trigger

ai_analysis: 18-Jul's defining feature is a split between a tightening curve and a soft outright: the cash–3M backwardation widened to +$7.50 across three sessions (the physical squeeze intensifying on −45.0% YTD stocks) even as the flat price consolidated near $3,150. The market is pricing prompt scarcity and outright caution at the same time.

The bear file keeps its best number of the year: 711 kt of Chinese exports in June — the first month ever above 700 kt — into an economy growing 4.3%, the slowest since 2022. The ex-China market is tightening on Gulf disruption exactly as China has record metal to send it. Both are true; the flat price must clear both.

The week's pivot was geopolitical: after the most intense US strikes since the June ceasefire, the flare-up eased Thursday evening and Qatar-brokered diplomacy (Vance/Kushner/Witkoff) is live against a ~18-Aug MoU. The strait is still shut to visible traffic — so the war premium is intact for now, but its durability is newly in question.

Alcoa's Q2 grounds the producer reality: record aluminium-segment margins (32.3%) and near-decade-high revenue, but an EPS miss on higher costs — including ~$35m more Section-232 on Canadian metal. The tariff is a double edge, inflating US premia while taxing cross-border flow. Balanced burden of proof into Monday's ring.
sources: Westmetall — LME official price & stock series (latest published 17-Jul)
CBS News — US-Iran war, ceasefire talks, Strait of Hormuz (16-Jul)
CSMonitor — Hormuz flare-up signals lasting fragility of US-Iran ceasefire
Reuters — China's June aluminium exports hit record high (711 kt)
S&P Global Platts — Japanese Q3 aluminum premium settles $395/mt CIF Japan

premium_explainer.desc: The physical-delivery adder over the LME price: regional logistics, duties/tariffs and local tightness — quoted $/t (or ¢/lb in the US) on top of LME cash. It is what converts an exchange price into a delivered price.

Q3-2026 settled at $395/t CIF Japan (Platts, 06-Jul) — a decade high and the fourth straight rise ($86 → $195 → $351.5 → $395). SMM flags spot tightness and squeeze risk under the LME's deep backwardation, firming CIF spot toward ~$365. Q4 talks (~Sep) open against a decade-high anchor and a still-disrupted strait — with US–Iran diplomacy (MoU to ~18-Aug) the swing factor.

Platts duty-unpaid last publicly marked ~$525–560 (mid $542.50, 03-Jul). The 6% EU duty, CBAM and Mozal's care & maintenance keep the structural floor; the war-risk freight component is easing at the margin as the US–Iran conflict de-escalates. The prospective 20% strait toll died on 14-Jul.

SMM's weekly DDP print ~109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t (10-Jul); Platts MW Transaction last printed 108.7¢ (03-Jul). The 50% Section-232 tariff-$ leg (≈$1,577 at the 17-Jul LME cash) dominates the all-in — US premia track Washington and the LME, not local tightness. Alcoa Q2 confirmed the tariff as a ~$35m q/q cost on its Canadian metal.

The decomposition below separates the mechanical tariff leg (Section-232 rate × LME) from the residual market premium. Most of the US 'premium' is a policy transfer, not a scarcity signal — and the new 20% strait toll extends the same logic to the freight leg of any Gulf-origin cargo, US-bound or not.

premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): Q3 settled $395 (decade high); CIF spot firming ~$365 on squeeze risk under the LME's deep backwardation. If the Hormuz disruption persists the spot holds near settlement and Q4 talks (~Sep) anchor high; a Qatar-brokered strait reopening around the ~18-Aug MoU reopens the path to the lower half of the $340–410 band.

European (EDU): last public Platts mark ~$525–560 (03-Jul). The 6% duty + CBAM floor plus Mozal's outage keep Europe structurally tighter than pre-war; the marginal driver — Gulf war-risk freight — is easing at the margin as strikes recede, with the 20% toll long dead.

US Midwest: SMM DDP ~109.75¢/lb (10-Jul); the tariff leg (50% × LME ≈ $1,577 at the 17-Jul cash) is ~two-thirds of the all-in, so US premia track the LME and Washington. Alcoa's Q2 flagged Section-232 as a ~$35m q/q cost on Canadian metal. Watch the tariff arithmetic and the next weekly, not the spot survey.

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$68 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$38, 45/3 grade); oversupply persists — Metro Mining shipped a RECORD 1.8 Mt in Q2 (+7% YoY; 2026 guidance 6.6–7.1 Mt held; SMM 08-Jul); Guinea–EGA settlement returns CBG flows while Guinea weighs export quotas toward a >$100/dmt target (SMM/Mining.com/Discovery Alert)

raw_materials.name: Caustic soda (flakes ≥98%, FOB NE Asia)
raw_materials.src: ~$500–510 FOB NE Asia; CFR Asia ~$380–560 — major refining reagent (ChemAnalyst / ChemTradeAsia)

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)
raw_materials.src: No liquid global index — largely regional/contract; indicative range (causticisation reagent)

alumina_outlook: Alumina: surplus intact — EGA's restart ramps through H2 while SHFE AO sits below its moving averages; expect ex-China consolidation in the $300–340 band (FOB Australia ~$338, API ~$303, Platts futures ~$308). SMM's June ex-China output dip (−6% y/y) and Guinea's quota push are the counterweights to watch.

Bauxite: Guinea oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$38); record Australian shipments add length. EGA's ramp returns buying to the seaborne market even as Guinea weighs a >$100/dmt export-price push.

Caustic soda / lime / freight: caustic stable (~$500/t FOB NE Asia). The delivered-cost leg is easing as the US–Iran conflict de-escalates, but war-risk cover on Gulf-bound reagent and alumina freight is still wider than pre-crisis. The commodity is cheap; insuring it into a Gulf refinery is less cheap than it was pre-July.

inputs.name: Calcined petroleum coke (CPC, anode-grade)
inputs.src: Peaked ~$896/t (2022); battery-anode demand + tight seaborne supply pressuring — CRU; limited price transparency

inputs.name: Coal tar pitch (CTP)
inputs.src: Anode binder; by-product of steel coke ovens — supply tied to steel output; thin spot market

inputs.name: Pre-baked anodes
inputs.src: Often produced in-house; China the swing exporter — mostly contract pricing

inputs.name: Electricity (smelter power)
inputs.src: Single largest cost & the competitiveness driver; no single index — PPAs / regional grid / self-gen. Energy complex firm: Brent ~$85.95 (+2.04% Fri, up >10% on the week on the Iran conflict); EU TTF gas ~€53/MWh — gas-indexed smelter power follows with a lag. Rising carbon-cost exposure.

inputs.name: Cryolite / bath makeup
inputs.src: Largely synthetic & recycled in-cell; limited spot market

inputs.name: Cathode / graphite blocks
inputs.src: Multi-year pot-reline cost; graphite-electrode supply (China) is the risk

inputs.name: Silicon metal
inputs.src: China Q2-26 ~$1,250 FOB; EU ~$1,580, US ~$2,625 (tariff split) — SMM/ChemAnalyst

inputs.name: Magnesium (99.9%)
inputs.src: China ~85% of global supply (~$2,359 Jan-26) — concentration risk; SMM/Asian Metal

inputs.name: Copper (LME)
inputs.src: LME-transparent leg of the input basket — Cu 3M $13,606.00 (16-Jul official, latest published); the complex firmed through the week (Cu +8.8% YTD). Busbar/cathode exposure prices off the LME.

inputs.name: Ti-B grain refiner (AlTi5B1)
inputs.src: Master-alloy rod; priced off titanium + conversion — mostly contract

logistics.name: Strait of Hormuz transit
logistics.val: Disrupted — shut to visible traffic; diplomacy live
logistics.note: After the most intense US strikes since the June ceasefire (mid-week), the flare-up eased Thursday evening and Qatar-brokered diplomacy (Vance/Kushner/Witkoff) is advancing toward reviving the MoU (Article 5 = reopen the strait, to ~18-Aug). But Lloyd's List reports no large vessels transited the southern (Omani) route with transponders on since 07-Jul, and Kpler shows crossings down sharply. ~80–85% of Gulf aluminium exports and ~60% of regional alumina imports use this waterway (CBS / CSMonitor / Lloyd's List).

logistics.name: Southern (Omani) corridor
logistics.note: The last workaround remains effectively unused: Lloyd's List cannot confirm any large-vessel transit with transponders on since 07-Jul (ships may be running dark). Oman's drafted two-corridor plan (southern free; northern under approval) is tied to the MoU's Article 5 implementation, which the current diplomacy targets (BOE Report / Lloyd's List).

logistics.name: Gulf war-risk insurance / surcharges
logistics.val: Wide but the acute spike is fading
logistics.note: War-risk quotes stay wide and short-validity, but the acute-escalation premium is receding as strikes ease and Qatar-brokered talks advance. The 20% US strait toll was withdrawn 14-Jul — keep the flat toll adder out of delivered-cost models; retain elevated insurance and interdiction/deviation risk until the strait is verifiably reopened (CNBC / The Hill).

logistics.name: Baltic Dry Index (BDI)
logistics.note: ~2,980 (latest public reference; dry-bulk demand from bauxite/alumina flows). BDI is a separate, slower-moving line from war-risk and blockade-enforcement costs — price the legs separately (Trading Economics / Baltic Exchange).

logistics.name: Land-bridge (Gulf → Jeddah / Red Sea)
logistics.note: With the strait still shut to visible traffic, overland trucking to Red Sea ports remains a live contingency, but the pressure eases as diplomacy advances toward reopening. Keep carrier capacity, costings and lead times current. Generic supply-chain observation for any Gulf-origin cargo.

logistics.name: Incoterms / demurrage
logistics.note: A strait still shut to visible traffic keeps force-majeure and deviation clauses live for any Gulf-routed cargo; the toll-allocation question died with the toll, but war-risk surcharge pass-through and interdiction/delay risk remain unsettled contract points to negotiate cargo-by-cargo until the waterway verifiably reopens. Generic supply-chain observation.

inputs_summary: Track closely (material AND tradeable): power (Brent ~$85.95, up >10% on the week; EU TTF ~€53 — gas-indexed power follows with a lag), calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency), silicon & magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated), copper (LME-transparent, 3M $13,606 at the 16-Jul official).
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B — no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.

Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (~85% China), CPC (Asia / battery-anode competition), AlF₃ (fluorspar/China). Secondary feed: the UAE aluminium scrap export ban and EU scrap-tariff changes keep secondary-feed tightness a rising cost-side theme (SMM).

Logistics is easing but not resolved: the commodity legs (alumina, bauxite) stay cheap and the 20% toll is dead, but the strait is still shut to visible traffic and war-risk cover remains wider than pre-July. Delivered-cost normalisation depends on the ~18-Aug MoU actually reopening the waterway.

long_term.label: Long-term — weekly (position degree)
long_term.writeup: Primary (weekly): unchanged count — the five-wave advance from the May-2025 low (~$2,300) terminated at $3,855 on 02-Jun-2026 (an extended, war-driven fifth wave with confirmed bearish weekly RSI divergence), and the ABC correction (A $3,359 / B $3,406 / C $3,061.5 on 02-Jul) bottomed inside the $2,950–$3,110 confluence where the .50 retracement overlaps the prior fourth of lesser degree. Base case (≈55%): a new impulse off $3,061.5 — the widening cash–3M backwardation (+$7.50) and −45.0% YTD stocks support it — with a weekly close above $3,260 (.382 trigger) opening $3,400 then $3,620 over the forecast year. Bull (≈20%): the Hormuz disruption entrenches and drives a direct run at the $3,800 prior high. Bear (≈25%): record Chinese exports and a diplomatic strait-reopening cap the rebound and force a retest of the $2,950–2,980 zone. Invalidation of the bullish count: a weekly close below $2,950. This week's flat-price consolidation with a tightening front end fits the base case; confidence moderate.

short_term.label: Short-term — daily (swing degree)
short_term.writeup: Swing (daily): from the 02-Jul low at $3,061.5 the market built a five-wave micro-impulse into $3,210.5 (wave i), corrected to the .50 retrace at $3,138 (wave ii, tagged and held on a settlement basis), and delivered the provisional wave-iii signature at Tuesday's $3,171 high with the cash–3M spread flipping to backwardation. The subsequent drift to the 17-Jul official at $3,146.5 is a shallow wave-iv-style pullback that has held the $3,138 shelf — and, critically, the backwardation WIDENED to +$7.50 through it, a tightening-into-weakness signature that favours the bulls. Base case (≈55%): iv holds $3,138, then wave-v probes $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382), where a close extends toward $3,330. Bear alternate (≈35%): a daily close below $3,138 opens $3,085/$3,061.5 — the read if a strait-reopening headline hits. Bull (≈10%): a gap-and-go straight through $3,260. Invalidation: daily close below $3,061.5. Confidence moderate — Monday's ring is the tell on whether the backwardation keeps building.

premium_drivers.t: Hormuz: strait shut to visible traffic (the dominant driver, now easing at the margin)

War-risk cover and interdiction/deviation risk remain the live premium drivers, though the acute-escalation spike is fading as strikes recede.

premium_drivers.t: Diplomacy is the new swing factor — MoU to ~18-Aug

A verified reopening is the fastest premium-unwind trigger; a breakdown with fresh strikes is the fastest escalation trigger.

premium_drivers.t: US Section-232 tariff at 50% — the mechanical leg of US premia
premium_drivers.d: The tariff-$ component of the US Midwest all-in is 50% × LME: ≈$1,577/t at the 17-Jul cash of $3,154 — roughly two-thirds of the ~$2,420 DDP premium. Alcoa's Q2 quantified the other edge: ~$35m more Section-232 cost q/q on its Canadian metal into the US. The tariff inflates US premia while taxing cross-border flow.

premium_drivers.t: EU duty + CBAM + Mozal keep the European floor
premium_drivers.d: The 6% MFN duty and CBAM carbon costs are structural adders under any Rotterdam duty-paid quote, and Mozal (~580 ktpa) remains on care & maintenance — Europe stays structurally tighter than pre-war even as the assessed premium drifts with the war-risk cycle.
src: European Commission — Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism

premium_settlement_src: S&P Global Platts Q3-2026 MJP settlement $395/t (assessed 06-Jul-2026), via IndexBox

so_what.line: Aluminium closed the week consolidating near $3,150 (17-Jul official cash $3,154, −0.5%), but the cash–3M backwardation widened to +$7.50 across three straight sessions on the thinnest stocks of the cycle (−45.0% YTD) — the front end is tightening even as the outright softens, while the US–Iran conflict pivots from strikes to Qatar-brokered diplomacy.
points: What moved: the 17-Jul LME official printed cash $3,154.00 (−$16, −0.50%) / 3M $3,146.50 (−$20, −0.63%) — net flat on the week (vs $3,156 a week earlier) after a $3,171 mid-week peak. The cash–3M spread WIDENED to +$7.50 backwardation (from +$3.50) and stock fell to 280,100 t. Markets closed for the weekend; next ring Mon 20-Jul.

Why it matters: three consecutive sessions of building backwardation on −45.0% YTD stocks is prompt-tightness intensifying — a tight-prompt / soft-outright configuration that usually resolves either up (if the squeeze persists) or down (if its driver, the Hormuz disruption, unwinds).

Geopolitics pivoted to diplomacy: after the most intense US strikes since the June ceasefire, the flare-up eased Thursday evening. Qatar is mediating and Vance/Kushner/Witkoff are working the channels toward the MoU (Article 5 = reopen the strait) that runs to ~18-Aug. The strait is still shut to visible traffic (no southern-route transits since 07-Jul, Lloyd's List); Brent rose to ~$85.95 (+2.04%, >10% on the week).

The producer read and the week ahead: Alcoa's Q2 posted record aluminium-segment margins (32.3%) and near-decade-high revenue but MISSED EPS ($2.12 vs ~$2.31) on higher costs incl. ~$35m more Section-232 on Canadian metal; shares fell. Watch: whether Monday's ring keeps the backwardation building, Norsk Hydro Q2 on 22-Jul, and the ~18-Aug MoU deadline on the strait.

exec_summary: The post-conflict correction extended but began to base. LME cash settled at $3,400.00/t on Friday 19 June, down 3.8% on the week from the prior Friday close of $3,536.00 and roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855. The week opened soft (cash $3,419.50 Monday), printed an intraweek low of $3,359.00 on Tuesday as the ceasefire narrative dominated, then stabilised in a $3,400-3,405 band through Wednesday-Friday. Aluminum still holds about +14% year-to-date and ~+33% year-on-year. Crude eased further as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) kept dollar-priced metals on the back foot - but the back-half stabilisation, not the headline drop, is the week's signal.

Under the flat-price weakness, the physical curve quietly re-tightened. The cash-to-3M spread dipped into a ~$30/t contango on Tuesday as de-escalation peaked, then flipped back to a +$2/t backwardation by Friday, while LME stocks drew a further ~4,600 t to ~315,300 t - a multi-year low near 1.3 days of global output. The US-Iran framework was formalised (MOU signed 17 June; official ceremony in Switzerland 19 June) and Hormuz is reopening, but gradually: 25 vessels transited Thursday, the most since April yet far below the ~110/day pre-war norm, and physical metal returns in months not weeks (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline). Net read: the war premium has largely unwound, but a persistent deficit and a slow restart are cushioning the downside - a two-sided, range-building market with sharp-rebound risk if the 60-day framework slips.

price_table.note: 19 Jun settlement; week low $3,359 (16 Jun), high $3,419.5 (15 Jun); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855
19 Jun official; tracks cash; back-half stabilisation in a $3,398-3,410 band
Dipped to ~-$30 contango (16 Jun) at peak de-escalation, flipped back to +$2 backwardation by 19 Jun - prompt tightness reasserting
19 Jun; -4,625 t on the week; multi-yr low ~1.3 days output; still drawing - deficit persists despite peace narrative
Q2 oversupply persists; energy relief from softer oil adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (11-yr high, +81% QoQ from Q1 $195); Q3 talks open but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran framework formalised: MOU signed 17 June (Versailles, post-G7), official ceremony in Switzerland Friday 19 June; mediator Pakistan confirmed the US port blockade ceases immediately and Tehran reopens Hormuz - the dominant catalyst that has now largely unwound the conflict premium.

Hormuz reopening is gradual, not instant: 25 commercial vessels transited on Thursday (the most since April) versus a ~110/day pre-war norm; transit requires registration for 'safe passage' (toll-free through the 60-day window), so physical flows normalise over weeks-to-months, not days.

Physical tightness reasserted under the headline: the cash-3M curve flipped from a mid-week ~$30 contango back to a +$2 backwardation by Friday, and LME stocks drew to ~315 kt (~1.3 days output, multi-year low) - the deficit is cushioning the flat-price downside.

Slow supply restart underpins the floor: EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline; Gulf pre-war supply was ~9% of global / ~25% of non-Chinese output, so the lost tonnage returns slowly even with the Strait open.

Compounding bears cap the upside: rising China output toward the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian smelter ramp, RUSAL diverting metal from China to Japan, and Guinea bauxite controls as a modest upstream offset; softer crude and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) round out the macro headwind.

implications.text: the flat-price correction has matured into a base-building range and the curve has re-tightened into backwardation, so prompt material retains a scarcity bid; use the stabilisation to layer disciplined forward hedges on priced tonnes rather than chasing fixed-price sales lower, and preserve value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA). Keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the 60-day framework slip.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 $350-353/t, an 11-year high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) but a confirmed, sustained Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 MJP settlements and renewal terms now, before normalisation and RUSAL's China-to-Japan diversion feed through to premiums.

softer ex-China alumina (~$298-300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; with vessels resuming Hormuz transit, begin a measured unwind of contingency-routing and war-risk insurance premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure - but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe passage (transit is rebounding but still well below pre-war volumes).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the credibility/mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit counts (the real normalisation tell), EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: Hormuz transit normalisation - whether daily vessel counts keep climbing toward the ~110/day pre-war norm and shipowners validate sustained safe passage; a stall would re-introduce a supply premium.

The cash-3M curve and LME stocks - continued draws and a holding/steepening backwardation would confirm the deficit persists despite the peace narrative; a flip back to contango would signal the unwind resuming.
Physical restart cadence - EGA/ALBA repair milestones gate how fast Gulf tonnage actually returns; the gap between visible and invisible inventories remains the key tightness risk.

Macro and competing supply - the 60-day framework's durability (slippage is the primary upside-price risk), China output against the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for USD direction.
weekly.sources: LME official cash/3-month settlements and warehouse stock via Westmetall (week of 15-19 June: cash $3,400.0, 3M $3,398.0, stock 315,300 t on 19 Jun); benchmark CFD price (~$3,400.85 on 19 Jun, ~+33% YoY), USD/DXY, energy and macro via Trading Economics; US-Iran MOU / Strait of Hormuz reopening, 17 June signing and 19 June Switzerland ceremony, and Thursday's 25-vessel transit via Al Jazeera, NBC News, PBS, Seatrade Maritime, CNN and the UK House of Commons Library (14-19 June); EGA/ALBA restart timelines and Gulf supply share via Trading Economics and Fastmarkets; MJP Q2 premium (~$350-353/t, +81% QoQ) and RUSAL China-to-Japan diversion via S&P Global Platts, Discovery Alert and AlCircle; alumina FOB Australia via Fastmarkets and S&P Global Platts.
weekly.caveat: Note: 19 June SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are estimates or most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
